<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In 2015, it was reported that Dylann Roof, the 21-year-old white supremacist who massacred<|8.84|> <|8.84|> nine members of Mother Emanuel African Methodist Episcopal Church in Charleston, South Carolina,<|14.72|> <|14.72|> was a member of the Evangelical Lutheran Church in America, a full communion partner of the<|20.42|> <|20.42|> Episcopal Church.<|22.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Elizabeth Eaton, presiding bishop of the ELCA, released a statement shortly after Roof's<|7.60|> <|7.60|> church membership was publicized, and it was made known that two of the black people he<|12.62|> <|12.62|> massacred had been educated in one of her denomination's seminaries.<|17.70|> <|17.70|> She says this:<|19.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> All of a sudden, and for all of us,<|3.88|> <|3.88|> this is an intensely personal tragedy.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> One of our own is alleged to have shot and killed<|9.78|> <|9.78|> two who adopted us as their own."<|12.62|> <|15.52|> What conceptualization of God,<|17.52|> <|18.42|> what idol had Dylann Roof formed about the divine<|23.42|> <|23.42|> that enabled him to do what he did five years ago this summer?<|27.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Did he conceive of God as being white like him, of being male like him, of being straight like him?<|8.50|> <|8.50|> Had God become so reduced, so small in his own mind, that he couldn't conceive of anything larger, freer, and more loving for himself and others by the time he'd gunned down those devout Bible study attendees?<|24.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What had he internalized about God sitting in the pews of his church as a child and young adult?<|7.00|> <|7.00|> Did he believe God was made in his own image instead of the other way around?<|13.00|> <|13.00|> What form of idolatry led him to do what he did?<|18.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Even more pressing, however, is what kind of religion, nation, community, or family produces this kind of outcome.<|10.00|> <|10.00|> What does it say about all of the influential institutions in his life that he was never at any point stopped in his tracks?<|20.00|> <|20.00|> What does it say about his upbringing, his family, his church?<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Growing up, my parents did a superb job of making sure that my brother and I thought critically about a variety of things:<|11.00|> <|11.00|> sports, politics, education, religion.<|16.00|> <|16.00|> I will never forget the time my mother was washing dishes and looked at me and said, "Broderick?"<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> How can people who claim to love God also be racist? How can they also hate us?<|9.00|> <|11.00|> I will not repeat how my father asked that question of us, but that's what my mother asked.<|18.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Over the course of my life, my mother has repeated this theodicy to us.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> It is a fair question, is it not, of how people who claim to love God cannot, for whatever reason,<|17.00|> <|17.00|> bring themselves to love those made in God's image.<|21.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> In that question, my mother demonstrates for all of us that God is best understood, not as an answer, but as a question.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> See, Dylann Roof and other white supremacists frame God as an answer.<|19.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> as a period at the end of a sentence.<|3.20|> <|4.40|> For my mother and others, God is malleable,<|7.48|> <|7.48|> dynamic, and molten, difficult to pin down.<|11.22|> <|13.14|> That malleability, dynamism, and molten-ness<|16.32|> <|16.32|> is what makes God beautiful.<|18.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> That is not usually the word one uses when referring to the divine, but it is fitting.<|8.98|> <|8.98|> There is an Arminian Orthodox theologian who has said he thinks of the first chapter of<|13.96|> <|13.96|> Genesis as a divine litany, as a divine back and forth, with God as liturgist and respondent.<|23.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God says, "This is good," or, "This is beautiful."<|6.50|> <|6.50|> The "this" of which God speaks is the universe, and all contained therein.<|14.24|> <|14.24|> But this beauty is only possible because there is a beautiful God singing the world into existence,<|22.56|> <|22.56|> calling forward great potential and love from the formless void.<|28.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The world in which we live, strangled on one hand by a global pandemic and on the other hand, the forces of white supremacy endemic to American society,<|14.00|> <|14.00|> fills like a formless void, does it not, and is longing for something beautiful and true.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> something that isn't easily pinned down, something that can't be comprehended or understood.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> The ancient Israelites and modern-day Jews rightfully say that this beautiful thing,<|16.00|> <|16.00|> this true thing of which we speak, they say that its name is unspeakable.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "I am who I am," this thing says to Moses at the burning bush.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> Early Christians called it the Holy Trinity, whose feast we celebrate today, and so does the church in the present time.<|17.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus called this source of energy "Father."<|5.00|> <|5.00|> Anglican theologians have called it "the ground of all being."<|10.00|> <|10.00|> A woman in my childhood church called it "a leaning post."<|15.00|> <|15.00|> However you name this beauty, this truth, this God,<|21.00|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Please never fool yourself into thinking that that's all there is.<|6.00|> <|6.00|> And sometimes, names are not as compelling as stories.<|12.00|> <|12.00|> A few days ago, I came across this post by a friend about herself, her husband, and her son,<|21.00|> <|21.00|> a fitting contemporary icon of the Trinity.<|25.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Quote, "Their relationship scares me a little. Fire and ice, these two. Some afternoons they<|12.88|> <|12.88|> lay entwined on the couch. You can't tell where one begins and the other ends. The air<|18.44|> <|18.44|> between them slack and easy. The bigger one lazily strums his guitar.<|24.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The other croons along, often multitasking.<|5.00|> <|6.00|> He investigates his father's body, his ears, his chest,<|9.90|> <|9.90|> and his belly button with complete disregard<|13.28|> <|13.28|> for personal space, connected, serene one.<|18.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Then there are other times.<|4.26|> <|4.26|> Times of flared nostrils, bulging eyes and tears.<|8.00|> <|8.00|> They are at war, the air wild and charged.<|11.22|> <|11.22|> He is the parent who administered tummy time, coached our son into walking, and still religiously<|17.62|> <|17.62|> cuts our son's fingernails.<|21.32|> <|21.32|> Potty training is fast approaching.<|23.42|> <|23.42|> Pray for us, she says.<|25.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> He stays focused, fueled by equal parts adoration and imagination for what is possible.<|8.34|> <|10.44|> Civil unrest is always imminent."<|13.34|> <|13.34|> Adoration and imagination.<|18.86|> <|20.38|> The Trinity.<|21.18|> <|22.52|> God.<|23.08|> <|24.30|> All gift.<|25.42|> <|25.42|> All gift.<|26.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Let us pray.<|6.18|> <|6.18|> God of delight, your wisdom sings your word at the crossroads where humanity and divinity<|12.88|> <|12.88|> meet.<|14.94|> <|14.94|> Invite us into your joyful being where you know and are known in each beginning, in all<|19.82|> <|19.82|> sustenance, in every redemption.<|22.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> that we may manifest your unity in the diverse work you entrust to us,<|5.44|> <|6.26|> truly reflecting your triune majesty in imagination that acts,<|10.84|> <|11.50|> in the hope that does not disappoint, and in the love that endures forever.<|16.22|> <|17.44|> Amen.<|17.82|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.10|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> "I am who I am," this thing says to Moses at the burning bush.<|8.00|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Jesus called this source of energy "Father."<|5.00|>

<|15.00|> However you name this beauty, this truth, this God,<|21.00|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Please never fool yourself into thinking that that's all there is.<|6.00|>
